Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed optative blessing clause: divine subject + future/volitive auxiliary + verb phrase; nominal compound zũ-noogo functions as outcome complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May things end well; may someone be protected, especially after danger, uncertainty, travel, illness, or a difficult errand.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yɛlã na n pʋg n sõma.
Usage Contexts: greetings, farewells, travel, illness recovery, family speech, religiously colored politeness
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common blessing formula tied to the central role of Wẽnd in Mossi social etiquette and the value placed on safe return and communal well-being.
First Example Sentence: Fʋ sẽn na n kẽng Ziniaré, Wẽnd na maan zũ-noogo.
Second Example Sentence: A ra lebga yir ne laafi, tɩ baaba yeel-a: Wẽnd maan zũ-noogo.
Third Example Sentence: Biigã sẽn pʋs n wa, ma yeela tɩ Wẽnd na maan zũ-noogo.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative equative proverb-like clause: noun subject + negative marker + nominal predicate; compact fixed expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Speech is not a tree; words can move quickly and should not be trusted as something fixed, silent, or harmless.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Gomdã tõe n yɩɩga, bɩ f tõog n gũus noorã.
Usage Contexts: advice, gossip, family disputes, workplace confidentiality, political talk
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects oral-society caution around reputation, testimony, and public speech in compounds, markets, and neighborhood gatherings.
First Example Sentence: Da gom yel-kãngã ne pʋ-peelem ye, noor ka tɩɩg ye.
Second Example Sentence: Yir pʋgẽ ninsaal bãmb n wʋmda fãa, noor ka tɩɩg ye.
Third Example Sentence: A sẽn da yeel kom-biisã, a yãame tɩ noor ka tɩɩg ye.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative possession clause: abstract noun subject + negative existential/possessive construction + object noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Death has no shame or respect for status; mortality reaches everyone regardless of wealth, age, or rank.
Usage Contexts: condolences, moral reflection, funerals, elder speech, religious discussion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on communal funeral discourse and the Mossi understanding that lineage, title, and wealth do not exempt anyone from destiny.
First Example Sentence: Naabã sẽn kʋʋmã, d fãa yãa tɩ kũum ka tar toogo ye.
Second Example Sentence: Da sɩnga n yõka f meng ye, kũum ka tar toogo ye.
Third Example Sentence: Pagã wilga biisã tɩ kũum ka tar toogo ye, bɩ b yõod yam.

Idiom (Mooré): Biiga ka yãa a ma sũuri ye.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative perception clause with kinship possessor: child subject + negative verb + possessed abstract body-emotion noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A child cannot fully understand a parent’s hidden worry, sacrifice, or emotional burden.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Biiga ka bãnga a ma sẽn tagsdã fãa ye.
Usage Contexts: family advice, parenting, storytelling, conflict mediation, elder-child instruction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in extended-family life where maternal care, food provision, and silent sacrifice are culturally respected but not always spoken openly.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Mooré): /bíːɡà ká jã́ː à má sṹːɾì jé/
First Example Sentence: Fʋ sẽn nong sukuu bala, biiga ka yãa a ma sũuri ye.
Second Example Sentence: A ma tʋmda yʋng ne zaabr, la biiga ka yãa a ma sũuri ye.
Third Example Sentence: Sã n lebga kãsem, f na n bãnga tɩ biiga ka yãa a ma sũuri ye.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative equative clause: abstract noun subject + comparative verb + concrete noun; elliptical proverb form.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Wisdom is more valuable than money; good judgment can protect a person where wealth cannot.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Bãngr ne tagsgo sõma yɩɩda arzɛka.
Usage Contexts: education, business, youth mentoring, dispute resolution, leadership advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects elder-centered education in Moogo, where experience, measured speech, and practical intelligence are valued above display of wealth.
First Example Sentence: Da maan ned ne ligdi bala ye; yam yɩɩda ligdi.
Second Example Sentence: Tʋʋmdã pʋgẽ, sẽn tar yam na n kõ sor sẽn tar ligdi.
Third Example Sentence: Kãsemã yeela kom-biisã tɩ yam yɩɩda ligdi.

Idiom (Mooré): Pʋgdb sãn teeme sida, biiga teeme yõodgo.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional biclausal proverb: subordinate sãn-clause + main clause; kinship subjects encode reciprocal social obligation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When relationships or social alliances change, dependent roles and joking obligations also change; adapt to the new social order.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sã ninsaalã zemsgo lebge, bɩ f me lebg f tʋʋmã.
Usage Contexts: kinship, joking relationships, marriage, clan relations, anthropology, elder discourse
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with Mossi kinship and joking-relationship norms, where affinal changes can reshape who may joke, tease, or claim obligations.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Mooré): /pʊ̀ɡdb sã́n téːmè sídà bíːɡà téːmè jṍːdɡò/
First Example Sentence: Pʋgdbã sãn teeme sida, biiga teeme yõodgo, bɩ da zãag f meng ye.
Second Example Sentence: A yeela ne wãnd tɩ pʋgdb sãn teeme sida, biiga teeme yõodgo.
Third Example Sentence: Yel-kãngã ka yelle bala ye; pʋgdb sãn teeme sida, biiga teeme yõodgo.

Idiom (Mooré): Zĩig sẽn ka tar zãabre ka kõ toogo ye.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-clause subject plus negative main predicate: place + relative modifier + negated verb phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A place or situation without effort, struggle, or preparation gives no lasting benefit.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Tʋʋmd ka be, yõod me ka be ye.
Usage Contexts: work ethic, farming, school, apprenticeship, entrepreneurship, motivational speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes agrarian Mossi experience: fields, rains, and harvests reward patient labor more than quick comfort.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Mooré): /zĩ́ːɡ sẽ́n ká táɾ zã́ːbɾè ká kṍ tóːɡʊ̀ jé/
First Example Sentence: F sẽn dat rɩɩb sõma, zĩig sẽn ka tar zãabre ka kõ toogo ye.
Second Example Sentence: Sukuudã pʋgẽ, zĩig sẽn ka tar zãabre ka kõ toogo ye.
Third Example Sentence: Raoogã tagsame tɩ zĩig sẽn ka tar zãabre ka kõ toogo ye, bɩ a tʋm.

Idiom (Mooré): Gomde n yita noorẽ, la paama tɩɩm yʋʋmẽ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated clause with contrastive temporal imagery: verbal noun subject + source phrase + delayed-result clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A word spoken quickly can have consequences much later; careless speech may return after a long time.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Gomdã tõe n wa ne yelle wʋsg n looge.
Usage Contexts: politics, legal testimony, family conflict, social media, diplomacy
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects the prestige of restrained speech before elders and chiefs, where a public statement can create long-term obligation or conflict.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Mooré): /ɡómde n jíta nɔ́ːrẽ́ lá páːma tɪ́ːm jʊ́ːmẽ́/
First Example Sentence: Da zãag n gom ye, gomde n yita noorẽ, la paama tɩɩm yʋʋmẽ.
Second Example Sentence: A noorã sẽn yɩɩg la woto, a bãnga tɩ gomde n yita noorẽ, la paama tɩɩm yʋʋmẽ.
Third Example Sentence: Tẽngã sẽn wʋm yel-kãngã, gomde n yita noorẽ, la paama tɩɩm yʋʋmẽ.

Idiom (Mooré): Sũur sẽn pʋʋsdã ka bʋgs ye.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-clause subject with negated stative predicate: heart + relative verb + negative adjective-like complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A heart that has forgiven is not heavy; forgiveness lightens anger and restores social peace.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sã f yafa nedã, f sũurã na n yɩ noogo.
Usage Contexts: reconciliation, family mediation, friendship, religious teaching, community peacebuilding
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Matches communal conflict-resolution values in households and neighborhoods, where public peace often matters more than private victory.
First Example Sentence: Yafa f zu-sobã, sũur sẽn pʋʋsdã ka bʋgs ye.
Second Example Sentence: Pagã yeela a biigã tɩ sũur sẽn pʋʋsdã ka bʋgs ye.
Third Example Sentence: D sẽn pʋʋs taaba, d yãa tɩ sũur sẽn pʋʋsdã ka bʋgs ye.

Idiom (Mooré): Ned ka zoe a sãab ye.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative transitive clause with possessed metaphorical object: human subject + negative verb + possessive noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person cannot escape the consequences of their own actions, history, or reputation.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ned ka tõe n bas a tʋʋma sẽn maana ye.
Usage Contexts: moral commentary, politics, family correction, public reputation, accountability
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the body-shadow image familiar in Sahelian everyday experience: wherever a person walks in the sun, their trace follows them.
First Example Sentence: A sẽn da maan yel-wẽnã, ned ka zoe a sãab ye.
Second Example Sentence: Politikã pʋgẽ, ned ka zoe a sãab ye, tɩ tẽngã n wʋmda fãa.
Third Example Sentence: A gomda ne pa-peelem, la ned ka zoe a sãab ye.
